title: Change in length under Dehn filling
speaker: David Futer

proof_stages.label: Length spectrum and commensurability problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines 'hearing' a hyperbolic 3-manifold via the real or complex length spectrum, reviews isospectral constructions and Sunada-type methods, and formulates commensurability as the possible positive remnant of the inverse spectral problem.

proof_stages.label: Short-geodesic mutation precursor
proof_stages.summary: Christian's earlier theorem constructs many mutant pretzel knot complements with equal volume, distinct commensurability classes, and the same first n+1 complex lengths, because very short geodesics have large collars and can be isotoped off the Conway mutation surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Large-length theorem and quantitative target
proof_stages.summary: The new joint theorem gives, for large n, a pair of noncommensurable mutant hyperbolic knot complements with equal volume whose length spectra agree up to length 2 log n plus bounded error and contain at least on the order of n^3/log n geodesics below that cutoff.

proof_stages.label: Long pseudo-Anosov tangle construction and block geometry
proof_stages.summary: The knots are built by gluing top and bottom tangles with incompressible boundary and no symmetries by a long pure pseudo-Anosov braid, then mutating one side; geometrically the complements are modeled by two caps joined by about n nearly identical mapping-torus product blocks.

proof_stages.label: Noncommensurability from canonical triangulations
proof_stages.summary: For arborescent choices the fine geometric triangulations are Epstein-Penner canonical, forcing symmetries and hidden symmetries to respect them and enabling known results of Neumann-Reid and Reid-Walsh to rule out commensurability between the mutant knot complements.

proof_stages.label: Short geodesics lie on one side of the mutation surface
proof_stages.summary: A geodesic that essentially intersects both the top and bottom tangles must either cross the long thick product region or take a cusp shortcut, yielding a lower bound of about 2 log n, so every geodesic below the cutoff can be homotoped to one side and retains its complex length after mutation.

proof_stages.label: Counting confined geodesics by pleated fibers
proof_stages.summary: Pleated fiber surfaces in the cyclic-cover model contribute about e^l/l curves of length at most l, and n nearly isometric copies can be used without overcounting, giving roughly n e^l/l geodesics and hence the advertised n^3/log n count when l=2 log n.

examples.name: Kac's drum question
examples.note: The classical inverse spectral question 'can you hear the shape of a drum?' is used as an analogy to motivate replacing the Laplacian spectrum of a planar drum by the length spectrum of a hyperbolic 3-manifold.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes Kac's 'can you hear the shape of a drum?' as the motivating question and redefines its terms for his setting, but does no work on Kac's original drum example.

passes.reason: a motivating spectral-rigidity question rather than a classifiable mathematical object

examples.name: Seventeen geodesics of length pi
examples.note: The hypothetical case of 17 distinct closed geodesics all having length pi makes concrete that the length spectrum is listed with multiplicity.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the length-spectrum-with-multiplicity definition to the hypothetical case, saying that 17 closed geodesics of length pi make pi appear 17 times.

examples.name: Vignéras arithmetic isospectral covers
examples.note: Vignéras's arithmetic construction is cited as an early source of distinct hyperbolic 3-manifolds with the same spectrum, illustrating the negative answer to the inverse spectral question.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites Vignéras's arithmetic construction of distinct isospectral covers only as historical context, without carrying out the construction.

passes.reason: arithmetic covers form systematic families and are cited as isospectral non-isometric examples refuting audible rigidity

examples.name: Finite nilpotent groups with almost-inner automorphisms
examples.note: In response to a question, finite nilpotent groups are mentioned as a good source of almost-conjugate but nonconjugate subgroups for applying Sunada's method.
examples.engagement_reason: He notes that finite nilpotent groups provide many almost-inner non-inner automorphisms useful for examples, but gives no concrete group or subgroup calculation.

examples.name: Plugging two log n into Margulis's geodesic asymptotic
examples.note: The computation that Margulis's generic asymptotic would give roughly n^4/4 geodesics at length 2 log n contextualizes how large the theorem's one-sided geodesic count is.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly plugs l = 2 log n into Margulis's geodesic asymptotic and computes the resulting growth as about n^4/4.

passes.reason: a heuristic substitution into an asymptotic formula rather than a standalone classifiable object
